description: distractor batch 5 - application of Code & Standards L3, GIPS overview, manager selection (121 items); parallel-weight distractors authored; correct key pinned to post-rotation layout
precondition: apply after batch_01, batch_02, rotate_drill_keys.py, batch_03, batch_04

options.A: to promote ethics and integrity and instill trust through the use of the GIPS standards by achieving universal demand for compliance by asset owners, adoption by asset managers, and support from regulators for the ultimate benefit of the global investment community.
options.B: The mission of the GIPS standards is to establish a mandatory global regulatory regime for performance reporting enforced by national securities regulators.
options.C: The mission is to guarantee the accuracy of every individual portfolio return a compliant firm reports to its clients.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'The mission of the GIPS standards is "to promote ethics and integrity and instill trust through the use of the GIPS standards by achieving universal demand for compliance by asset owners, adoption by asset managers, and support from regulators for the ultimate benefit of the global investment community."'
rationales.B: Incorrect. GIPS compliance is voluntary and ethics-driven — the mission is universal demand for compliance, not government enforcement.
rationales.C: Incorrect. GIPS governs composite-level presentation standards; it does not certify each portfolio-level number.

options.A: Any organization involved in financial services, including custodians and consultants, may claim GIPS compliance for the services it provides.
options.B: Only firms managing more than $1 billion in discretionary assets are eligible to claim compliance.
options.C: only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with GIPS Standards for Firms.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The claim is available to investment management firms and asset owners managing assets on a discretionary basis and competing for business — not service providers generally.
rationales.B: Incorrect. No asset-size threshold exists for claiming compliance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with GIPS Standards for Firms; asset owner that manages investments directly and/or through external managers on behalf of participants/beneficiaries/organization itself but does not compete for business would comply with GIPS Standards for Asset Owners; consultants, software houses, or third-party performance measurement providers such as custodians may not claim to be GIPS-compliant; GIPS compliance cannot be claimed for only some of a firm's products, nor for specific composites, pooled funds, or portfolios — compliance can be achieved only on a firm-wide basis.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with the GIPS Standards for Firms. (An asset owner that manages investments, directly and/or through the use of external managers, on behalf of participants, beneficiaries, or the organization itself — but does not compete for business — would comply with the GIPS Standards for Asset Owners.) Consultants, software houses, or third-party performance measurement providers such as custodians may not claim to be GIPS-compliant. GIPS compliance cannot be claimed for only some of an investment firm's products, nor for specific composites, pooled funds, or portfolios; compliance can be achieved only on a firm-wide basis.'

options.A: The GIPS standards use 'prospective client' and 'prospective investor' interchangeably to mean anyone who requests marketing materials.
options.B: prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether person/entity currently invests with firm through another strategy.
options.C: A prospective investor is any person who has expressed interest in a segregated account managed to one of the firm's strategies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The terms are distinct — one attaches to segregated-account strategies, the other to pooled funds.
rationales.B:
Full statement: prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether person/entity currently invests with firm through another strategy; prospective investor is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's pooled funds and qualifies to invest in the pooled fund, again irrespective of any other current investments with firm.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the context of the GIPS Standards for Firms, a prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of the firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether the person or entity currently invests with the firm through another strategy that the firm offers. A prospective investor is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of the firm's pooled funds and qualifies to invest in the pooled fund, again irrespective of any other current investments with the firm.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. That describes a prospective CLIENT; a prospective INVESTOR is one interested in investing in a POOLED FUND.

options.A: super-regional bank whose wealth management department has two separate divisions: private client division (Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals/families) and institutional client division (Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profits including pension funds/charitable foundations, does not solicit non-institutional business).
options.B: The Eastern National Bank case illustrates that a bank must always define the GIPS firm as the entire holding company, including lending operations.
options.C: The case shows that two divisions marketing under different names must always be combined into a single GIPS firm.

rationales.A:
Full statement: super-regional bank whose wealth management department has two separate divisions: private client division (Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals/families) and institutional client division (Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profits including pension funds/charitable foundations, does not solicit non-institutional business); each division has own investment team, traders, marketing, admin, accounting; institutional division decides to achieve GIPS compliance, private client division decides not to; institutional division may become GIPS-compliant because it holds itself out as distinct business unit with own autonomous investment management, research, trading, and administrative team.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Consider the case of a super-regional bank whose wealth management department consists of two separate and distinct divisions: the private client division and the institutional client division. The private client division, called Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals and families. The institutional client division, called Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profit organizations including pension funds and charitable foundations; it does not solicit or handle non-institutional business. Each division has its own investment management team, traders, marketing department, administrative personnel, and accounting department. After a few years of operating in this manner, the institutional investment unit decides to achieve compliance with the GIPS standards, but the private client division makes a business decision not to implement the GIPS standards. The institutional client division may nonetheless be in position to become GIPS-compliant because it holds itself out to customers as a distinct business unit, with its own autonomous investment management, research, trading, and administrative team.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The firm is defined as the entity held out to clients — the wealth-management business units — not the whole bank.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Divisions held out to the market as distinct businesses may each be defined as separate GIPS firms.

options.A: Total firm assets include only discretionary, fee-paying portfolios eligible for composites.
options.B: phrase refers to aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which firm has investment management responsibility.
options.C: Total firm assets are measured at historical cost to avoid valuation subjectivity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The aggregate covers ALL assets under management responsibility — discretionary or not, fee-paying or not.
rationales.B:
Full statement: phrase refers to aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which firm has investment management responsibility; total firm assets include assets managed by sub-advisors that firm has authority to select but do NOT include advisory-only assets or uncalled committed capital.

Correct. Per the reading: 'It also establishes the boundaries for determining total firm assets. The phrase total firm assets refers to the aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which a firm has investment management responsibility. Total firm assets include assets managed by sub-advisors that the firm has authority to select but do not include advisory-only assets or uncalled committed capital.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The measure is aggregate FAIR VALUE, not cost.

options.A: A firm may restate its historical composite performance to reflect a new corporate structure after a reorganization.
options.B: GIPS is silent on corporate reorganizations, leaving historical treatment to each firm's discretion.
options.C: firm that has been defined for purposes of GIPS standards may undergo subsequent changes in corporate structure or organizational design.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Changes in structure or design do not permit alteration of historical composite performance.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The standards address this directly: history is preserved through organizational change.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm that has been defined for purposes of GIPS standards may undergo subsequent changes in corporate structure or organizational design; changes in firm's organization are NOT permitted to lead to alteration of historical performance; general rule: apart from correcting errors, historical performance is not to be altered.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A firm that has been defined for the purposes of the GIPS standards may very well undergo subsequent changes in its corporate structure or organizational design. Changes in a firm's organization are not permitted to lead to alteration of historical performance, however. Indeed, we may put it down as a general rule that, apart from correcting errors, historical performance is not to be altered.'

options.A: GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite.
options.B: Inclusion in composites is optional for segregated accounts, which firms may present individually to prospects.
options.C: Only the firm's ten largest discretionary accounts must be assigned to composites.

rationales.A:
Full statement: GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite; discretionary, fee-paying pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition; key term is 'discretionary' although GIPS standards do not define term itself; generally, portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy; although both discretionary and non-discretionary portfolios are included in total firm assets, only discretionary portfolios are included in composites; if client restrictions impede investment process to such extent that strategy cannot be implemented as intended, may be presumed to render portfolio non-discretionary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite. Discretionary, fee-paying pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition. A key term in this requirement is "discretionary," although the GIPS standards do not define the term itself. Generally speaking, a portfolio is discretionary if the manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy... Although both discretionary and non-discretionary portfolios are included in total firm assets, only discretionary portfolios are included in composites. If the client imposes restrictions on the manager's freedom to make investment decisions to buy, hold, and sell securities so as to carry out the investment strategy and achieve the portfolio's financial objectives, then the manager must consider whether the portfolio is in fact discretionary. In general, restrictions that impede the investment process to such an extent that the strategy cannot be implemented as intended may be presumed to render the portfolio non-discretionary, and it should not be included in a composite.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. ALL discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite — cherry-picked individual presentation is what the rule prevents.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The requirement is universal across qualifying accounts, not size-ranked.

options.A: When GIPS standards conflict with local law, the GIPS standards prevail because compliance is a voluntary higher standard.
options.B: A firm facing a conflict between GIPS and local law must abandon its compliance claim entirely.
options.C: when GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding calculation and presentation of performance, GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations AND to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Firms must FOLLOW THE LAW and disclose the conflict — local law prevails over GIPS provisions.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Compliance continues; the firm complies with law and DISCLOSES the conflict.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding calculation and presentation of performance, GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations AND to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report; firms are strongly encouraged to comply with GIPS standards in addition to applicable regulatory requirements.

Correct. Per the reading: 'When the GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding the calculation and presentation of performance, the GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations and to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report. Firms are strongly encouraged to comply with the GIPS standards in addition to applicable regulatory requirements.'

options.A: Money-weighted returns are never permitted under the GIPS standards, which mandate time-weighted returns universally.
options.B: Firms may freely choose between MWR and TWR for any portfolio based on which presents more favorably.
options.C: firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if firm has control over external cash flows AND: (1) portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment OR (2) illiquid investments are significant part of investment strategy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. MWRs are permitted where the firm controls external cash flows and the portfolio meets the closed-end/fixed-life-type criteria.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The choice is conditioned on cash-flow control and portfolio characteristics — never on favorable optics.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if firm has control over external cash flows AND: (1) portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment OR (2) illiquid investments are significant part of investment strategy; annualized, since-inception MWRs must be calculated at least annually; for periods beginning with effective date of 2020 GIPS Standards, daily external cash flows must be used (external cash flows for periods prior to this date must be reflected on at least a quarterly basis); portfolios for which MWR is calculated must be valued at least annually and as of period end for which performance is calculated.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if the firm has control over the external cash flows and: (1) the portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment or (2) illiquid investments are a significant part of the investment strategy. Annualized, since-inception MWRs must be calculated at least annually. For periods beginning with the effective date of the 2020 GIPS Standards, daily external cash flows must be used. (External cash flows for periods prior to this date must be reflected on at least a quarterly basis.) Portfolios for which a money-weighted return is calculated must be valued at least annually and as of the period end for which performance is calculated.'

options.A: true TWR (intra-period valuation) = 0.41%: sub-period 1 return = (109,000 - 10,000 - 100,000)/100,000 = -0.01.
options.B: The Modified Dietz method and true time-weighted return always produce identical results when a mid-period contribution occurs.
options.C: The example shows the return cannot be computed because the contribution date splits the measurement period.

rationales.A:
Full statement: true TWR (intra-period valuation) = 0.41%: sub-period 1 return = (109,000 - 10,000 - 100,000)/100,000 = -0.01; sub-period 2 return = (110,550 - 109,000)/109,000 = 0.0142; TWR = (1 + -0.01)(1 + 0.0142) - 1 = 0.0041 = 0.41%; Modified Dietz weight = (30-5)/30 = 0.83; Modified Dietz = (110,550 - 100,000 - 10,000)/(100,000 + 10,000 × 25/30) = 550/108,333.33 = 0.51%; Modified IRR also gives 0.51%.

Correct. Per the reading: 'consider a portfolio with a beginning value of $100,000 as of 31 May, a value of $109,000 on 5 June (which includes a cash contribution of $10,000 received that day), and an ending value of $110,550 on 30 June... The portfolio's true time-weighted return using the intra-period valuation method is 0.41%: rt,1 = (109,000 − 10,000 − 100,000)/100,000 = −0.01; rt,2 = (110,550 − 109,000)/109,000 = 0.0142; rtwr = (1 + −0.01)(1 + 0.0142) − 1 = 0.0041 = 0.41%.' And: 'wi = (CD − Di)/CD = (30 − 5)/30 = 25/30 = 0.83. Applying the Modified Dietz formula to the same example gives a return of 0.51%: rModDietz = (110,550 − 100,000 − 10,000)/[100,000 + (10,000 × 25/30)] = 0.0051 = 0.51%.' 'Applying the Modified IRR method to the simple example used earlier in this section gives a result of 0.51%, the same as the rate of return found with the Modified Dietz method.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The methods differ precisely because Modified Dietz approximates without intra-period valuation — the example contrasts the two results.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Intra-period valuation at the cash-flow date is exactly how the true TWR handles the split.

options.A: The top of the valuation hierarchy is the firm's internal model price, since firms know their holdings best.
options.B: Quoted prices for SIMILAR investments in active markets sit at the top of the recommended hierarchy.
options.C: objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date (must be used if available).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Model prices sit far down the hierarchy; objective observable market prices come first.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Similar-investment quotes are the NEXT level; identical-investment quoted prices in active markets top the hierarchy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date (must be used if available); this is the top of the hierarchy and takes precedence over all other valuation approaches.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The valuation must be determined using the objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for an identical investment in an active market on the measurement date, if available.' This is Level 1 of the valuation hierarchy — objective, observable, unadjusted quoted price for identical investment in active market.

options.A: A single firm-wide valuation hierarchy must be applied identically to every composite and pooled fund without exception.
options.B: Valuation hierarchies are required only for private-market composites, not liquid strategies.
options.C: GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The recommendation is that hierarchies be appropriate to — and may be specific to — individual composites or pooled funds.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Documentation of valuation policies applies across the firm; the composite-specific recommendation is not confined to private markets.
rationales.C:
Full statement: GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific; for investment strategies employing plain-vanilla securities trading in robust markets, quoted prices are readily available; other composites may represent strategies that materially make use of securities that trade infrequently in relatively illiquid markets where values must be imputed or estimated (real estate and private equity obvious examples).

Correct. Per the reading: 'the GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that the valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific. Normally, for investment strategies that employ plain-vanilla securities trading in robust markets, quoted prices are readily available. Other composites, however, may represent strategies that materially make use of securities that trade infrequently in relatively illiquid markets where values must be imputed or estimated. Real estate and private equity are obvious examples.'

options.A: once established, valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request.
options.B: Valuation policies are internal working documents that need not be shared with prospective clients.
options.C: Firms may vary valuation policies period to period as market conditions warrant, without documentation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: once established, valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request; for assets valued using quantitative models, useful to list input factors such as discount rates and risk-adjusted cash flow projections and to review basis for estimating them; portfolio managers, security analysts, quantitative analysts, and traders should participate in these discussions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Once established, the valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request. For assets valued using quantitative models, it is useful to list input factors such as discount rates and risk-adjusted cash flow projections and to review the basis for estimating them. Portfolio managers, security analysts, quantitative analysts, and traders should participate in these discussions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Policies must be made AVAILABLE TO PROSPECTIVE CLIENTS upon request.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Once established, policies must be documented and applied CONSISTENTLY.

options.A: Composite returns are computed as the simple arithmetic average of the constituent portfolio returns.
options.B: Composite returns weight each portfolio by its ending-period market value.
options.C: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ V_0,pi)] where r_C is composite return, r_pi is return of individual portfolio i, V_0,pi is beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V_0,pi is total beginning fair value of all individual portfolios in composite.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Equal weighting ignores portfolio size; the method asset-weights the constituent returns.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The formula shown weights by BEGINNING-of-period values, not ending values.
rationales.C:
Full statement: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ V_0,pi)] where r_C is composite return, r_pi is return of individual portfolio i, V_0,pi is beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V_0,pi is total beginning fair value of all individual portfolios in composite; composite return is sum of individual portfolio returns weighted in proportion to their respective percentages of aggregate beginning assets.

Correct. Per Equation 7: 'Under the "beginning assets" weighting method, the composite return calculation is shown in Equation 7: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ_pi=1^n V_0,pi)] where rC is the composite return, rpi is the return of an individual portfolio i, V0,pi is the beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V0,pi is the total beginning fair value of all the individual portfolios in the composite. In other words, the composite return is the sum of the individual portfolio returns weighted in proportion to their respective percentages of aggregate beginning assets.'

options.A: to prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite.
options.B: Composites need include only portfolios whose clients have consented to performance disclosure.
options.C: Composite membership is determined annually by the firm's performance committee based on representativeness.

rationales.A:
Full statement: to prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite; all actual, fee-paying, discretionary pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition; non-discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds must NOT be included in composites; non-fee-paying discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds may be included but additional disclosures may be required; if a strategy is offered as a pooled fund and a segregated account, the pooled fund must be included in a composite for that strategy; firm does not need to create a composite for a strategy if offered only as a pooled fund.

Correct. Per the reading: 'To prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, the GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite. All actual, fee-paying, discretionary pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition. Non-discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds must not be included in composites. Non-fee-paying discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds may be included in a composite, but additional disclosures may be required... If a strategy is offered as a pooled fund and a segregated account, the pooled fund must be included in a composite for that strategy. The firm does need not to create a composite for a strategy if it is offered only as a pooled fund.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Inclusion is mandatory for all actual, fee-paying, discretionary portfolios meeting the definition — client consent is not a filter.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Definition-based inclusion, not committee selection, prevents showcasing only the best performers.

options.A: some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries.
options.B: Any client-imposed ESG restriction automatically renders the portfolio non-discretionary and excludes it from composites.
options.C: ESG restrictions are ignored for discretion purposes because they reflect values rather than investment constraints.

rationales.A:
Full statement: some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries; others might have restrictions that prohibit sale of company stock; additionally, legal restrictions may apply (e.g., public fund might be statutorily precluded from investing in non-domestic securities); NONE of these constraints automatically renders a portfolio non-discretionary; portfolio manager must determine whether client-imposed constraints are likely to materially affect ability to execute investment strategy; if not material, could include in composite with unrestricted portfolios; if material, may include in composite with similarly constrained portfolios or classify as non-discretionary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries. Other clients might have restrictions that prohibit the sale of company stock. Additionally, legal restrictions may apply. For instance, a public fund might be statutorily precluded from investing in non-domestic securities. None of these constraints automatically renders a portfolio non-discretionary. Rather, in these and other cases, the portfolio manager must determine whether the client-imposed constraints are likely to materially affect her ability to execute the investment strategy. If the constraints are determined to not have a material effect, the manager could include the portfolio in a composite with portfolios that have no such restriction. If the constraint is material, the manager may include the portfolio in a composite with other, similarly constrained portfolios or classify it as non-discretionary and exclude the portfolio from all composites.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Restrictions are evaluated for their MATERIALITY to strategy implementation — they do not automatically remove discretion.
rationales.C: Incorrect. ESG restrictions are genuine constraints; the question is whether they materially impede the intended strategy.

options.A: Legacy low-basis holdings have no bearing on the discretion assessment because all portfolios hold legacy positions.
options.B: client could direct that legacy holdings with low cost basis must not be sold because of adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains.
options.C: A do-not-sell direction on legacy holdings always renders the entire portfolio discretionary, since the firm manages everything else freely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client-directed no-sell constraints can materially impede strategy implementation and thus bear directly on discretion.
rationales.B:
Full statement: client could direct that legacy holdings with low cost basis must not be sold because of adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains; retaining asset in portfolio may skew performance; if holding assets hinders manager's ability to implement intended strategy, either entire portfolio should be considered non-discretionary and excluded from firm's composites OR individual assets should be removed and remaining assets for which manager has full discretion should be included in composite; alternately, firm might include materiality threshold enabling it to consider portfolio discretionary if non-discretionary assets consist of less than certain percentage of portfolio assets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A client could insist that the manager retain specific holdings that might or might not otherwise be held in a portfolio. For example, the client could direct that legacy holdings with a low cost basis must not be sold because of the adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains. In such cases, retaining the asset in the portfolio may skew performance, and — whether the impact is favorable or unfavorable in any given measurement period — the outcome would not reflect the results of the manager's actual discretionary investment management. If holding the assets hinders the manager's ability to implement the intended strategy, either the entire portfolio should be considered non-discretionary and excluded from the firm's composites or the individual assets should be removed and the remaining assets for which the manager has full discretion should be included in the composite. Alternately, the firm might include a materiality threshold in its policy, enabling it to consider a portfolio discretionary if the non-discretionary assets consist of less than a certain percentage of portfolio assets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The effect depends on materiality — a large frozen position can make the portfolio non-discretionary.

options.A: one possible hierarchy: Investment Mandate → Asset Classes → Style or Strategy → Benchmarks → Risk/Return Characteristics.
options.B: Composite definition hierarchies must begin with benchmarks, since the benchmark defines the strategy.
options.C: The GIPS standards prescribe a single mandatory hierarchy that all firms must apply without adaptation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: one possible hierarchy: Investment Mandate → Asset Classes → Style or Strategy → Benchmarks → Risk/Return Characteristics; composite based on investment mandate bears summary product/strategy description like 'Global Equities'; composite based on asset class (equity or fixed income) may be acceptable; asset-class modifier may indicate composite's investment style or strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One possible hierarchy that may be helpful for the firm considering how to define composites is outlined as follows. Investment Mandate; Asset Classes; Style or Strategy; Benchmarks; Risk/Return Characteristics. A composite based on the investment mandate bears a summary product or strategy description, such as "Global Equities"... A composite based on the constituent portfolios' asset class, such as "equity" or "fixed income," may also be acceptable; however, asset classes are broadly inclusive, and because generic descriptions are not very informative, asset class composites should be offered only if they are legitimately and meaningfully representative of the firm's products.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The illustrated hierarchy runs mandate → asset class → style/strategy → benchmark → risk/return — benchmarks come later, not first.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The hierarchy is a POSSIBLE approach; firms adapt composite definitions to their own structures.

options.A: Style assignment must rely exclusively on the manager's self-declared strategy label.
options.B: Portfolios may not be assigned to style categories because styles are marketing constructs.
options.C: portfolio may be assigned to one of style categories based on money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of portfolio's holdings.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Assignment can be grounded in the portfolio's actual holdings characteristics (or returns-based methods), not self-labels.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Style-based composite definition is legitimate; money-weighted holdings characteristics are one recognized assignment basis.
rationales.C:
Full statement: portfolio may be assigned to one of style categories based on money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of portfolio's holdings; e.g., portfolio holding stocks with average market cap of $5 billion along with relatively high price-to-earnings multiple, relatively high price-to-book ratio, and relatively low dividend yield would likely be identified as mid-cap growth portfolio; alternately, portfolio's historical monthly or quarterly returns might be regressed against returns of pertinent capital market indexes to determine which style-specific benchmarks best explain portfolio's performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A portfolio may be assigned to one of the style categories based on the money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of the portfolio's holdings. For example, a portfolio holding stocks with an average market capitalization of $5 billion along with a relatively high price-to-earnings multiple, a relatively high price-to-book ratio, and a relatively low dividend yield, would likely be identified as a mid-cap growth portfolio. Alternately, the portfolio's historical monthly or quarterly returns might be regressed against the returns of pertinent capital market indexes to determine which style-specific benchmarks best explain the portfolio's performance.'

options.A: Composite definition should begin by surveying what competitors present, to ensure comparability.
options.B: (1) review firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified.
options.C: The common-sense approach starts by defining as many composites as possible to maximize marketing flexibility.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The starting point is the firm's OWN organizational structure and investment process.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) review firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified; (2) review firm's existing marketing materials to determine which strategies are offered as segregated account; also review competitors' materials and RFPs; (3) construct provisional framework using descriptive captions to identify possible composites; (4) test how well firm's fee-paying, discretionary portfolios would fit provisional framework; (5) review proposed set to ensure they satisfy GIPS requirements in aggregate; (6) document composite definitions in detail and circulate for final review.

Correct. Per the reading: '1. Review the firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified... 2. Review the firm's existing marketing materials to determine which strategies are offered as a segregated account. If possible, review marketing materials from competitors and recently received requests for proposals (RFPs) to determine how the industry defines products similar to those the firm offers. 3. Construct a provisional framework using descriptive captions to identify possible composites. 4. Taking into consideration the clients' investment policies, test how well the firm's fee-paying, discretionary portfolios would fit the provisional framework... 5. Review the proposed set of composites to ensure that, in the aggregate, the composites are likely to satisfy the requirements of the GIPS standards. 6. Document the composite definitions in detail and circulate the definitions for final review by all affected parties within the firm.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Proliferating composites invites cherry-picking; the process identifies genuinely distinctive strategies.

options.A: Benchmark-based composite definitions are prohibited because benchmarks change over time.
options.B: Risk/return-based composite definitions are permitted only for hedge fund strategies.
options.C: firms may also define composites based on portfolios' benchmarks, as long as benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and firm has no other composites with same characteristics.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Benchmark-based definition is permitted when the benchmark reflects the strategy and no other composite dimension separates the portfolios.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Risk/return characteristics are a general definitional basis, not a hedge-fund carve-out.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firms may also define composites based on portfolios' benchmarks, as long as benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and firm has no other composites with same characteristics; this approach particularly appropriate if portfolios are limited to holding stocks that are held in the index; portfolios sharing distinctive risk/return profiles may reasonably be grouped together — for example, enhanced index funds with benchmark-specific targeted excess returns and tracking error tolerances might fall into natural groups; fixed-income composites can be meaningfully defined by asset classes/market segments (government debt, mortgage-backed, convertibles, high-yield), investment strategies (fundamental credit analysis, sector rotation, interest rate anticipation), or investment styles (indexing, core-plus).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Firms may also define composites based on the portfolios' benchmarks, as long as the benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and the firm has no other composites with the same characteristics. This approach is particularly appropriate if the portfolios are limited to holding stocks that are held in the index. Finally, portfolios sharing distinctive risk/return profiles may reasonably be grouped together. For example, enhanced index funds with benchmark-specific targeted excess returns and tracking error tolerances might fall into natural groups. Fixed-income composites can likewise be meaningfully defined in many dimensions. For example, composites might conform to asset classes or market segments such as government debt, mortgage-backed securities, convertible bonds, or high-yield bonds; investment strategies such as fundamental credit analysis, sector rotation, or interest rate anticipation; or investment styles such as indexing or core-plus.'

options.A: GIPS standards require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after portfolio comes under management.
options.B: New portfolios enter composites at the firm's discretion, typically when performance stabilizes.
options.C: New portfolios must wait a mandatory twelve months before composite inclusion.

rationales.A:
Full statement: GIPS standards require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after portfolio comes under management; firms required to establish, document, and consistently apply policy of including new portfolios in appropriate composites on timely basis; for many strategies, new portfolios should be included as of beginning of next full performance measurement period after firm receives funds; if composite returns are calculated monthly and portfolio is funded on 20 May, composite should include new portfolio as of beginning of June; may take time to invest assets particularly when portfolio funded in kind or when securities to be purchased are relatively illiquid; GIPS standards give firms some discretion — firm must establish policy on composite-by-composite basis and apply it consistently.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards governing composite construction require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after the portfolio comes under management. Firms are required to establish, document, and consistently apply a policy of including new portfolios in the appropriate composites on a timely basis. For many strategies, new portfolios should be included as of the beginning of the next full performance measurement period after the firm receives the funds. If composite returns are calculated monthly and a portfolio is funded on 20 May, the composite should include the new portfolio as of the beginning of June. It may take time to invest the assets of a new portfolio in accordance with the desired investment strategy, however, particularly when the portfolio is funded in kind... or when the securities to be purchased are relatively illiquid (e.g., in emerging markets). Accordingly, the GIPS standards give firms some discretion.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Inclusion must follow a TIMELY AND CONSISTENT policy — waiting for good performance is the abuse the rule prevents.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No twelve-month mandate exists; the firm's documented policy governs, applied consistently.

options.A: Terminated portfolios are removed from all historical composite periods to keep the composite representative of current accounts.
options.B: firm must include terminated portfolio in historical performance of appropriate composite through last full measurement period in which firm had discretion to manage portfolio to the strategy.
options.C: Terminated portfolios remain in the composite through the termination date, including the final partial period.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Removing terminated accounts from history creates survivorship bias — history must retain them.
rationales.B:
Full statement: firm must include terminated portfolio in historical performance of appropriate composite through last full measurement period in which firm had discretion to manage portfolio to the strategy; in many cases, firm loses discretion over portfolio upon being notified of pending termination; if firm calculates performance monthly and is informed on 20 May that management contract is being terminated effective 31 May and is instructed to stop trading forthwith, firm should include portfolio in composite only through 30 April; historical performance of terminated portfolios remains in the appropriate composite.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Under the GIPS standards, a firm must include a terminated portfolio in the historical performance of the appropriate composite through the last full measurement period in which the firm had the discretion to manage the portfolio to the strategy. In many cases, the firm loses its discretion over the portfolio upon being notified of a pending termination... When the firm being terminated loses its discretion over the portfolio, it should include the portfolio in the composite through the last full measurement period prior to notification of termination. To use the same example, if a firm that calculates performance monthly is informed on 20 May that its management contract is being terminated effective 31 May and is instructed to stop trading forthwith, then the firm should include the portfolio in its composite only through 30 April.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Inclusion runs through the last FULL measurement period under management, not the partial final period.

options.A: Portfolios may be moved between composites at the firm's discretion to improve composite representativeness.
options.B: Once assigned, a portfolio may never change composites for any reason.
options.C: GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Switching requires DOCUMENTED changes in the portfolio's mandate or a composite redefinition — discretionary rebalancing of membership is prohibited.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Switches are possible on documented mandate changes or composite redefinition; the bar is on undocumented moves.
rationales.C:
Full statement: GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate; historical performance of the portfolio must remain with the original composite; this is important requirement because if firms could transfer portfolios from one composite to another at will, unethical firm might identify and exploit opportunities to improve reported performance of selected composites by re-populating them with portfolios whose investment results were most advantageous during measurement period.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in the portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate. The historical performance of the portfolio must remain with the original composite. This is an important requirement; if the GIPS standards permitted firms to transfer portfolios from one composite to another at will, an unethical firm might identify and exploit opportunities to improve the reported performance of selected composites by re-populating them with the portfolios whose investment results were most advantageous during the measurement period.'

options.A: (1) portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if client revises mandate, objective, or strategy governing investment of portfolio assets and guideline changes are documented (e.g., client might decide to modify portfolio mandate from mid-cap value to large-cap value, or from domestic equity to global equity, while retaining same investment advisor).
options.B: Reassignment between composites is permitted whenever a portfolio's returns diverge meaningfully from the composite average, since persistent divergence indicates the portfolio no longer represents the strategy.
options.C: Reassignment of a portfolio from one composite to another requires the prior written approval of the firm's independent verifier, who must confirm that the move does not distort either composite's record.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if client revises mandate, objective, or strategy governing investment of portfolio assets and guideline changes are documented (e.g., client might decide to modify portfolio mandate from mid-cap value to large-cap value, or from domestic equity to global equity, while retaining same investment advisor); (2) portfolio can be reassigned to another composite if the original composite is redefined in such way that portfolio no longer fits it (redefinition of existing composite should be highly unusual event; generally, if strategy changes over time, most appropriate to create new composite).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards describe two conditions under which portfolios can be reassigned. First, a portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if the client revises the mandate, objective, or strategy governing the investment of portfolio assets and the guideline changes are documented... Second, a portfolio can be reassigned to another composite if the original composite is redefined in such a way that the portfolio no longer fits it. Generally, if a strategy changes over time, it is most appropriate to create a new composite; accordingly, the redefinition of an existing composite should be a highly unusual event.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Performance divergence is not a ground; a client-revised mandate or composite redefinition is required.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No verifier-approval condition exists; documentation of the qualifying change is what is required.

options.A: A significant cash flow is any external flow exceeding one percent of composite assets, as fixed by the GIPS standards.
options.B: significant cash flow is client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent firm from implementing the strategy.
options.C: Significant cash flows are firm-initiated rebalancing transfers between portfolios in the same composite.

rationales.A: Incorrect. No fixed percentage is prescribed; the firm defines significance ex ante, composite by composite.
rationales.B:
Full statement: significant cash flow is client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent firm from implementing the strategy; significant cash flows are more likely to be an issue for strategies such as fixed income and emerging markets, wherein liquidity of underlying securities inhibits ability to quickly invest incoming cash or to conduct orderly sale of portfolio securities to meet cash outflow; firms must define 'significant' on ex ante, composite-specific basis and must consistently follow composite-specific significant cash flow policy; in event of significant cash flows, portfolio may be temporarily removed from composite; firms may alternatively use temporary new accounts to remove effect.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the event of significant cash flows, a portfolio may be temporarily removed from the composite. A significant cash flow is a client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent the firm from implementing the strategy. Significant cash flows are more likely to be an issue for strategies such as fixed income and emerging markets, wherein the liquidity of the underlying securities inhibits the ability to quickly invest the incoming cash or to conduct an orderly sale of portfolio securities to meet a cash outflow. Firms must define "significant" on an ex ante, composite-specific basis and must consistently follow the composite-specific significant cash flow policy. Alternatively, firms may use temporary new accounts to remove the effect of a significant cash flow.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The concept concerns CLIENT-DIRECTED external flows large enough to disrupt strategy implementation.

options.A: sample statement: 'Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate.
options.B: Sample switching policies authorize moves whenever the portfolio manager judges a better composite fit exists.
options.C: Policy language on composite switching is discouraged because each move is unique.

rationales.A:
Full statement: sample statement: 'Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate. The portfolio shall be removed from the original composite at the end of the last calendar month before the event causing the removal occurred and shall be added to the appropriate new composite at the beginning of the calendar month following the date on which the portfolio is substantially invested. The historical performance of the portfolio shall remain in the original composite.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Here is a sample statement for a policy: "Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate. The portfolio shall be removed from the original composite at the end of the last calendar month before the event causing the removal occurred and shall be added to the appropriate new composite at the beginning of the calendar month following the date on which the portfolio is substantially invested. The historical performance of the portfolio shall remain in the original composite."'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Manager judgment is not a basis; the sample language conditions moves on redefinition or documented client changes.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Written policy language is exactly what the standards contemplate — the sample statement exists for that reason.

options.A: GIPS Composite Reports must present a minimum of ten full years of annual performance from the very first report, so newer composites cannot produce a compliant report until a decade of history exists.
options.B: A single year of annual performance is sufficient for a new composite's first GIPS Composite Report, with no obligation to extend the record in subsequent presentations.
options.C: for each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, GIPS standards require firms to show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The initial minimum is FIVE years (or since inception if shorter), BUILDING to ten.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Five years (or since-inception) is the floor, then annual additions up to ten.
rationales.C:
Full statement: for each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, GIPS standards require firms to show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented; if composite has been in existence for less than 5 years, firm may present returns since inception and build over time to 10 years of required returns.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, the GIPS standards require that firms show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless the composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that the GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented. If the composite has been in existence for less than 5 years, the firm may present returns since inception and build over time to the 10 years of required returns.'

options.A: Internal dispersion disclosure is recommended but optional for composites with fewer than twenty portfolios.
options.B: GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of returns earned by individual portfolios in composite be presented.
options.C: Internal dispersion refers to the tracking error of the composite against its benchmark.

rationales.A: Incorrect. An annual internal dispersion measure is REQUIRED (with limited exceptions for very small composites, e.g., five or fewer portfolios).
rationales.B:
Full statement: GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of returns earned by individual portfolios in composite be presented; requirement intended to allow users to see how consistently firm implemented its strategy across individual portfolios; wide range of results should prompt recipient to inquire about possible causes of variability of returns to portfolios purportedly managed in accordance with same strategy; may suggest composite is defined too broadly; only portfolios in composite for entire year are included in calculation of this dispersion measure; GIPS standards do not limit firms to one measure of internal dispersion — method chosen should fairly represent range of returns for each annual period.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of the returns earned by individual portfolios in the composite be presented. This important requirement is intended to allow users to see how consistently the firm implemented its strategy across individual portfolios. A wide range of results should prompt the recipient of the performance presentation to inquire about possible causes of the variability of returns to portfolios that are purportedly managed in accordance with the same strategy. It may suggest, among many other possibilities, that the composite is defined too broadly to provide meaningful information... (Note that only those portfolios in the composite for the entire year are included in the calculation of this dispersion measure.)... Note that the GIPS standards do not limit firms to using one of the measures of internal dispersion described here.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Internal dispersion measures the spread of INDIVIDUAL PORTFOLIO returns within the composite, not benchmark-relative risk.

options.A: Ex post standard deviation requirements were removed from the 2020 GIPS standards as duplicative.
options.B: The variability disclosure covers the composite only; benchmark variability is the index provider's responsibility.
options.C: 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms require that firms present information about historical variability of composite and benchmark returns.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The 2020 standards RETAIN the historical-variability presentation requirement (the three-year annualized ex post standard deviation).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Variability information covers composite AND benchmark returns.
rationales.C:
Full statement: 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms require that firms present information about historical variability of composite and benchmark returns; where monthly composite returns are available, firms must present, as of each annual period end, three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of the monthly returns of both the composite and the benchmark; rationale is to give prospective clients an indication of risk of investment strategy as executed by firms under consideration; because all GIPS Composite Reports include same risk measure and that measure is based on historical experience rather than subjective inputs, GIPS standards allow for some degree of comparability among firms claiming compliance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms also require that firms present information about the historical variability of composite and benchmark returns. Specifically, the GIPS standards state that, where monthly composite returns are available, firms must present, as of each annual period end, the three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of the monthly returns of both the composite and the benchmark. The rationale is to give prospective clients an indication of the risk of an investment strategy as executed by the firms under consideration. Because all GIPS Composite Reports include the same risk measure, and that measure is based on historical experience rather than subjective inputs, the GIPS standards allow for some degree of comparability among firms that claim compliance.'

options.A: sample: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards.
options.B: Compliance statements are free-form; firms draft whatever language conveys their compliance claim.
options.C: The compliance statement may appear only in documents reviewed by CFA Institute before publication.

rationales.A:
Full statement: sample: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Spinning Top Investments has been independently verified for the periods 1 January 2011 to 31 December 2020. The verification report is available upon request. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. Verification does not provide assurance on the accuracy of any specific performance report.'

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 1: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Spinning Top Investments has been independently verified for the periods 1 January 2011 to 31 December 2020. The verification report is available upon request. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. Verification does not provide assurance on the accuracy of any specific performance report.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Prescribed compliance language exists — the sample statement reflects the required wording for verified/unverified claims.
rationales.C: Incorrect. CFA Institute does not pre-review firm reports; the firm is responsible for correct use of the statement.

options.A: Net-of-fees returns must be computed using each client's actual negotiated fee, never a model fee.
options.B: per Exhibit 8 disclosure: net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return.
options.C: Model-fee net returns are computed using the firm's LOWEST fee schedule to show best-case results.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Model fees are permitted; the exhibit's method deducts 1/12th of the highest applicable fee monthly.
rationales.B:
Full statement: per Exhibit 8 disclosure: net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return; gross-of-fees returns are presented before management and custodial fees but after all trading expenses; composite and benchmark returns are presented gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes.

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 7: 'Gross-of-fees returns are presented before management and custodial fees but after all trading expenses. Composite and benchmark returns are presented gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes. Net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Using the lowest fee would overstate net performance; the HIGHEST applicable fee is the conservative model input.

options.A: performance from past firm or affiliation may be linked to performance of new or acquiring firm if new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements — apply on composite-specific basis: (1) substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new or acquiring firm.
options.B: Past performance always transfers with individual portfolio managers as their personal track record.
options.C: Performance portability is prohibited under the GIPS standards; every firm starts its record at inception.

rationales.A:
Full statement: performance from past firm or affiliation may be linked to performance of new or acquiring firm if new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements — apply on composite-specific basis: (1) substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new or acquiring firm; (2) decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within new or acquiring firm; (3) new or acquiring firm has records that document and support reported performance; (4) there must be NO break in the track record between past firm or affiliation and new or acquiring firm.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The "portability" of past performance is a complex and sometimes contentious subject. Performance from a past firm or affiliation may be linked to the performance of the new or acquiring firm if the new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements. The requirements, which apply on a composite-specific basis, are that (1) substantially all the investment decision makers are employed by the new or acquiring firm, (2) the decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within the new or acquiring firm, (3) the new or acquiring firm has records that document and support the reported performance, and (4) there must be no break in the track record between the past firm or affiliation and the new or acquiring firm.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Performance belongs to the FIRM; linking requires the portability conditions (decision-makers, process, supporting records, continuity).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Portability is permitted — conditionally — when the specified requirements are met.

options.A: A break in the track record permanently bars any use of the prior firm's performance history.
options.B: if there is break in track record between past firm and new or acquiring firm, AND if first three portability tests are met, then performance from past firm or affiliation may be USED TO REPRESENT the historical performance of new or acquiring firm.
options.C: A break in the track record may be bridged by interpolating returns across the gap.

rationales.A: Incorrect. With the other portability tests met, the prior history may still be USED (shown) — it just cannot be LINKED to the new record.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if there is break in track record between past firm and new or acquiring firm, AND if first three portability tests are met, then performance from past firm or affiliation may be USED TO REPRESENT the historical performance of new or acquiring firm — but the two performance records may NOT be linked.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If there is a break in the track record between the past firm and the new or acquiring firm, and if the first three portability tests are met, then the performance from the past firm or affiliation may be used to represent the historical performance of the new or acquiring firm — but the two performance records may not be linked.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Gaps are never filled by interpolation; the records stand unlinked on either side of the break.

options.A: Acquired non-compliant assets must be brought into compliance before the acquisition closes.
options.B: Acquired assets remain permanently exempt from GIPS compliance as legacy business.
options.C: if GIPS-compliant firm acquires another firm or affiliation, the firm is given a one-year 'grace period' to bring any non-compliant assets into compliance for future reporting periods.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The standards allow a ONE-YEAR grace period after the acquisition for bringing non-compliant assets into compliance.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exemption is temporary — one year — not permanent.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'If a GIPS-compliant firm acquires another firm or affiliation, the firm is given a one-year "grace period" to bring any non-compliant assets into compliance for future reporting periods.'

options.A: per Exhibit 8 disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.'.
options.B: The Spinning Top example demonstrates that performance from a prior affiliation may be presented without any accompanying disclosure once the portability requirements are satisfied and the records are linked.
options.C: The example demonstrates that whenever an investment team changes firms, its composite history transfers automatically to the new employer without any conditions on process, personnel, or supporting records.

rationales.A:
Full statement: per Exhibit 8 disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.'; disclosure b: 'Spinning Top investments acquired the composite through an acquisition of ABC Capital in May 2014. Firm assets prior to 2014 are not presented because the composite was not part of the firm.'

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.' And footnote b: 'Spinning Top investments acquired the composite through an acquisition of ABC Capital in May 2014. Firm assets prior to 2014 are not presented because the composite was not part of the firm.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exhibit's disclosure language exists precisely because linked prior performance must be disclosed as such.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The link rests on the portability conditions — decision-makers and process substantially intact, records available.

options.A: verification may be characterized as process in which independent expert assesses firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of requirements of GIPS standards.
options.B: Verification is a mandatory annual audit required to maintain any claim of GIPS compliance.
options.C: Verification certifies the accuracy of each composite's reported returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: verification may be characterized as process in which independent expert assesses firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of requirements of GIPS standards; verification is intended to provide firm and users of its GIPS Reports greater confidence in its claim of compliance with GIPS standards; verification does NOT provide assurance as to accuracy of any particular composite or pooled fund presentation; GIPS standards RECOMMEND that firms undergo verification (voluntary).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Verification may be informally and unofficially characterized as a process in which an independent expert assesses a firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of the requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification is intended to provide the firm and the users of its GIPS Reports greater confidence in its claim of compliance with the GIPS standards. Verification does not provide assurance as to the accuracy of any particular composite or pooled fund presentation... The GIPS standards recommend that firms undergo verification.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Verification is VOLUNTARY (though recommended); compliance can be claimed without it, with required disclosure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Verification assesses firm-wide POLICIES AND PROCEDURES for composite construction and GIPS-consistent presentation — not each return's accuracy.

options.A: A performance examination is simply the formal name given to the standard verification engagement once the verifier's report has been issued to the firm.
options.B: Performance examinations are mandatory annual engagements for a compliant firm's five largest composites, measured by composite assets at each year-end.
options.C: firm that is verified may choose to have detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Examination is a distinct, DETAILED engagement on specific composites or pooled funds, available only in addition to verification.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Examinations are optional and firm-selected, not size-mandated.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm that is verified may choose to have detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite; performance examination is separate from verification (which is firm-wide) and applies to specific composite(s) or pooled fund(s).

Correct. Per the reading: 'A firm that is verified may choose to have a detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite.'

options.A: verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards.
options.B: Verifiers confine their work to recalculating a sample of composite returns.
options.C: Verifiers rely on management's representations without independent understanding of firm operations.

rationales.A:
Full statement: verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards; verifiers must not only obtain copy of firm's GIPS-related policies and procedures but also ensure that all applicable policies and procedures are properly included and adequately documented; finally, verifiers must understand policies, procedures, and methodologies the firm uses to value portfolios and compute investment performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards. Verifiers must not only obtain a copy of the firm's GIPS-related policies and procedures but also ensure that all applicable policies and procedures are properly included and adequately documented. Finally, verifiers must understand the policies, procedures, and methodologies the firm uses to value portfolios and compute investment performance.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Verifiers must UNDERSTAND the firm — structure, operations, policies, procedures, and methodologies — beyond recalculation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Independent understanding and testing, not bare reliance on representations, define the engagement.

options.A: Verification requires testing every portfolio and composite at the firm without exception.
options.B: although verification is conducted on firm-wide basis, verifiers may use sampling methodology to conduct required testing.
options.C: Sampling is prohibited in verification because it leaves untested accounts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Verification is firm-wide in SCOPE, but verifiers may use sampling to conduct the required testing.
rationales.B:
