Full statement: although verification is conducted on firm-wide basis, verifiers may use sampling methodology to conduct required testing; when selecting sample portfolios, verifiers must take into consideration number of composites and pooled funds at firm and number of portfolios in each composite; in addition, verifiers must take into account total assets under management, internal control structure at firm, number of years being verified, systems used in construction and maintenance of composites, method of calculating performance, and whether firm uses external performance measurement services; if verifier encounters errors or discovers that firm's record-keeping is deficient, larger sample or additional verification procedures may be warranted.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Although verification is conducted on a firm-wide basis, verifiers may use a sampling methodology to conduct the required testing. When selecting sample portfolios, verifiers must take into consideration the number of composites and pooled funds at the firm and the number of portfolios in each composite. In addition, verifiers must also take into account the total assets under management, the internal control structure at the firm, the number of years being verified, the systems used in the construction and maintenance of composites, the method of calculating performance, and whether the firm uses external performance measurement services. The selection of sample accounts for testing is a critical step in the verification process. If the verifier encounters errors or discovers that the firm's record-keeping is deficient, a larger sample or additional verification procedures may be warranted.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Sampling methodologies are expressly permitted.

options.A: Verification provides no substantive benefit to the firm beyond supplying marketing language for pitch books and requests for proposals submitted to institutional prospects.
options.B: The only benefit of undergoing verification is the satisfaction of securities-regulatory requirements that apply to firms presenting performance to prospective clients.
options.C: in addition to making claim of compliance on firm-wide basis more credible, verification process may benefit firm in other ways: increased knowledge in performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Benefits include improved internal knowledge of the standards, stronger policies and procedures, and credibility of the compliance claim.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Verification is not a regulatory requirement; its benefits are credibility and process improvement.
rationales.C:
Full statement: in addition to making claim of compliance on firm-wide basis more credible, verification process may benefit firm in other ways: increased knowledge in performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages; above all, verification supports guiding principles of fair representation and full disclosure of investment performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In addition to making the claim of compliance on a firm-wide basis more credible, however, the verification process may benefit the firm in other ways: increased knowledge in the performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages. Above all, verification supports the guiding principles of fair representation and full disclosure of investment performance.'

options.A: Qualitative analysis of manager selection consists of examining the manager's historical returns and attribution statistics.
options.B: Qualitative and quantitative analysis are interchangeable labels for the same due-diligence work.
options.C: investment due diligence which evaluates the manager's investment process (philosophy, process, people, portfolio), and operational due diligence which evaluates the manager's infrastructure and firm (process/procedure, firm, investment vehicle, terms, monitoring).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Returns and attribution are QUANTITATIVE work; the qualitative side is investment due diligence (philosophy, process, people, portfolio) plus operational due diligence.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The two are distinct components — qualitative evaluates process and operations; quantitative evaluates the return record.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'The qualitative analysis consists of investment due diligence, which evaluates the manager's investment process, and operational due diligence, which evaluates the manager's infrastructure and firm.' And Exhibit 1: 'Investment due diligence: Which manager "best" fits the portfolio need? Is the manager expected to continue to generate this return distribution? Philosophy... Process... People... Portfolio... Operational due diligence: Is the manager's track record accurate, and does it fully reflect risks? Process and procedure... Firm... Investment vehicle... Terms... Monitoring.'

options.A: Manager due diligence assumes that past outperformance mechanically repeats in future periods.
options.B: Due diligence assumes manager returns are random, making process evaluation pointless.
options.C: a consistent, robust investment process will generate a similar return distribution relative to risk factors through time, assuming underlying dynamics of the market have not dramatically changed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The assumption is about PROCESS consistency generating a similar return distribution relative to risk factors — not naive performance persistence.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The premise is the opposite: a robust, consistent process produces evaluable, repeatable risk-relative behavior.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a consistent, robust investment process will generate a similar return distribution relative to risk factors through time, assuming underlying dynamics of the market have not dramatically changed; one important goal of manager due diligence is to understand whether manager's investment process, people, and portfolio construction satisfy this assumption — that is, will investment process generate expected return from expected sources.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An underlying assumption of investment manager due diligence is that a consistent, robust investment process will generate a similar return distribution relative to risk factors through time, assuming the underlying dynamics of the market have not dramatically changed. One important goal of manager due diligence is to understand whether the manager's investment process, people, and portfolio construction satisfy this assumption — that is, will the investment process generate the expected return from the expected sources?'

options.A: Every manager search examines the identical full universe regardless of what prompted the search.
options.B: new search based on strategic or tactical view (adding new strategy or risk exposure) will examine broad universe of comparable managers and select best within universe.
options.C: Universe scope is set by the consultant's database coverage rather than the purpose of the search.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Scope varies with purpose — a new strategic/tactical exposure scans broadly; a replacement search is narrower and profile-driven.
rationales.B:
Full statement: new search based on strategic or tactical view (adding new strategy or risk exposure) will examine broad universe of comparable managers and select best within universe; adding manager to increase capacity or diversification within strategy already held will look for complement to current holdings; replacing single manager in particular strategy will look for best manager within strategy universe; IPS in part determines what relative terms 'best,' 'complement,' and 'cost/benefit' mean.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A new search based on a strategic or tactical view, such as adding a new strategy or risk exposure, will examine a broad universe of comparable managers and look to select the best within the universe. Adding a manager to increase capacity or diversification within a strategy already held will look for a complement to current holdings. Replacing a single manager in a particular strategy will look for the best manager within the strategy universe. The IPS in part determines what the relative terms "best," "complement," and "cost/benefit" mean.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The SEARCH PURPOSE, not database convenience, determines how wide the net is cast.

options.A: Manager selection starts from the null hypothesis that the manager IS skillful until performance proves otherwise.
options.B: typically starts with the null hypothesis (hypothesis assumed to be true until demonstrated otherwise) that the manager is NOT skillful.
options.C: Hypothesis testing has no role in manager selection because skill cannot be framed statistically.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The null is that the manager is NOT skillful; evidence must overturn it — the reverse framing invites Type I errors.
rationales.B:
Full statement: typically starts with the null hypothesis (hypothesis assumed to be true until demonstrated otherwise) that the manager is NOT skillful; if this null is rejected when correct, Type I error; if this null is not rejected when incorrect, Type II error.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The determination of whether a manager is skillful typically starts with the null hypothesis (the hypothesis assumed to be true until demonstrated otherwise) that the manager is not skillful.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The framework is explicitly a hypothesis test with the no-skill null.

options.A: Decision makers weight Type I and Type II errors identically because both carry the same economic cost.
options.B: Type II errors receive more attention because missed opportunities are psychologically painful.
options.C: psychologically people seek to avoid feelings of regret — Type I errors are mistaken rejections whereas Type II errors are failures to detect true relationship.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Attention is asymmetric — and the reasons are psychological as much as economic.
rationales.B: Incorrect. It is TYPE I errors (acting on a mistaken hire/retention) that draw focus: errors of commission generate regret; unnoticed Type II omissions do not.
rationales.C:
Full statement: psychologically people seek to avoid feelings of regret — Type I errors are mistaken rejections whereas Type II errors are failures to detect true relationship; Type I errors create explicit costs, Type II errors create opportunity costs — individuals put less weight on opportunity costs; Type I errors relatively straightforward to measure and often directly linked to decision maker's compensation; Type I errors more transparent to investors so entail regret plus pain of explaining to investor; Type II errors less transparent unless investor tracks fired managers.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Decision makers appear predisposed to worry more about Type I errors than Type II errors. Potential reasons for this focus on Type I errors are as follows: Psychologically, people seek to avoid feelings of regret. Type I errors are mistaken rejections, whereas Type II errors are failures to detect a true relationship. As a result, Type I errors create explicit costs, whereas Type II errors create opportunity costs. Because individuals appear to put less weight on opportunity costs, Type I errors are psychologically more painful than Type II errors. Type I errors are relatively straightforward to measure and are often directly linked to the decision maker's compensation... Type I errors are more transparent to investors, so they entail not only the regret of an incorrect decision but the pain of having to explain this decision to the investor.'

options.A: cost driven by size, shape, mean, and dispersion of return distributions of skilled and unskilled managers within universe.
options.B: Error costs depend only on the fee differential between active and passive alternatives.
options.C: Type I and Type II error costs are fixed constants independent of the manager universe.

rationales.A:
Full statement: cost driven by size, shape, mean, and dispersion of return distributions of skilled and unskilled managers within universe; smaller the difference in sample size and distribution mean and wider dispersion of distributions, smaller expected cost of Type I or Type II error; more efficient markets likely exhibit smaller differences in distributions of skilled and unskilled managers indicating lower opportunity cost of retaining and lower cost of hiring an unskilled manager.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The cost is driven by the size, shape, mean, and dispersion of the return distributions of the skilled and unskilled managers within the universe. The smaller the difference in sample size and distribution mean and the wider the dispersion of the distributions, the smaller the expected cost of the Type I or Type II error. More efficient markets are likely to exhibit smaller differences in the distributions of skilled and unskilled managers, indicating a lower opportunity cost of retaining and the lower the cost of hiring an unskilled manager.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Fees matter, but the costs are driven by the return DISTRIBUTIONS — size, shape, mean, and dispersion — of skilled versus unskilled managers.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Costs vary with the composition of the universe; distributions determine what each error type forgoes or incurs.

options.A: Returns-based style analysis examines each portfolio holding individually to classify the manager's style.
options.B: RBSA is a qualitative interview technique for understanding a manager's stated philosophy.
options.C: top-down approach that involves estimating portfolio's sensitivities to security market indexes representing a range of distinct factors.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Holding-by-holding examination is HOLDINGS-based analysis; RBSA is top-down, regressing returns on style-factor indexes.
rationales.B: Incorrect. RBSA is quantitative — estimating return sensitivities to market indexes representing distinct factors.
rationales.C:
Full statement: top-down approach that involves estimating portfolio's sensitivities to security market indexes representing a range of distinct factors; adds analytical step of estimating risk factors as opposed to using third-party or self-reported style categorization; straightforward, typically does not require large amount of additional or difficult to acquire data; identifies important drivers of return and risk factors for period analyzed; can be estimated even for complicated strategies; comparable across managers and through time; use of returns data provides objective style check not subject to window dressing; can be run immediately after data available.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Returns-based style analysis (RBSA) is a top-down approach that involves estimating a portfolio's sensitivities to security market indexes representing a range of distinct factors. Although RBSA adds the additional analytical step of estimating the risk factors, as opposed to using a third-party or self-reported style categorization, the analysis is straightforward and typically does not require a large amount of additional, or difficult to acquire, data. RBSA should identify the important drivers of return and risk factors for the period analyzed and can be estimated even for complicated strategies. In addition, the process is comparable across managers and through time, and the use of returns data provides an objective style check that is not subject to window dressing.'

options.A: RBSA is imprecise tool; accuracy may be compromised because RBSA effectively attributes performance to unchanging average portfolio during period.
options.B: RBSA's chief disadvantage is that it requires full position-level transparency from the manager.
options.C: RBSA has no meaningful disadvantages, which is why it has displaced holdings-based analysis.

rationales.A:
Full statement: RBSA is imprecise tool; accuracy may be compromised because RBSA effectively attributes performance to unchanging average portfolio during period; limits ability to identify impact of dynamic investment decisions and may distort decomposition across sources of added value; portfolio being analyzed might not reflect current or future portfolio exposures; if portfolio contains illiquid securities, stale prices may understate risk exposure of strategy — particular problem for PE and VC managers.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The disadvantage is that RBSA is an imprecise tool. Although the additional computational effort required is not onerous, accuracy may be compromised, because RBSA effectively attributes performance to an unchanging average portfolio during the period. This attribution limits the ability to identify the impact of dynamic investment decisions and may distort the decomposition across sources of added value. Furthermore, the portfolio being analyzed might not reflect the current or future portfolio exposures. If the portfolio contains illiquid securities, stale prices may understate the risk exposure of the strategy. This is a particular problem for private equity (PE) and venture capital (VC) managers that hold illiquid or non-traded securities. VC and PE firms report performance based on the internal rate of return of cash distributions and appraisals of ongoing projects. As a result, reported performance can understate the volatility of return for shorter horizons or time periods with limited liquidity events.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Needing only returns is RBSA's ADVANTAGE; the disadvantage is imprecision — it attributes performance to an unchanging average portfolio.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Imprecision and averaging effects are real limitations; the approaches are complements, not substitutes.

options.A: Holdings-based style analysis has no disadvantages because it uses actual positions.
options.B: HBSA's only weakness is that it cannot detect style drift.
options.C: computational effort increases with complexity of strategy and depends on timing and degree of transparency provided by manager.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Computational burden grows with strategy complexity, and results depend on the timing and degree of manager transparency.
rationales.B: Incorrect. HBSA detects drift well; its weaknesses are effort, data timing, and transparency dependence.
rationales.C:
Full statement: computational effort increases with complexity of strategy and depends on timing and degree of transparency provided by manager; challenging for hedge fund, PE, and VC managers averse to or unable to provide position-level pricing; even with mutual funds, necessary transparency may come with time lag; usefulness may be compromised because portfolio reflects snapshot in time and might not reflect portfolio going forward particularly for high-turnover strategies; some factors difficult to estimate if strategy is complex; typically easier with equity strategies particularly ETFs (holdings published daily); if portfolio has illiquid securities, stale pricing may underestimate risk exposure.

Correct. Per the reading: 'As with RBSA, HBSA has some disadvantages. The computational effort increases with the complexity of the strategy and depends on the timing and degree of the transparency provided by the manager. This extra effort can be challenging for hedge fund, private equity, and venture capital managers that may be averse to or unable to provide position-level pricing. Even with mutual funds, the necessary transparency may come with a time lag. The usefulness of the analysis may be compromised because the portfolio reflects a snapshot in time and might not reflect the portfolio going forward, particularly for high-turnover strategies. Some factors may be difficult to estimate if the strategy is complex because HBSA requires an understanding of the underlying strategy. In general, HBSA is typically easier with equity strategies, particularly for ETFs because holdings are published daily. If the portfolio has illiquid securities, stale pricing may underestimate the risk exposure of the strategy.'

options.A: results of RBSA and HBSA should be tracked over time in order to ascertain if risk trends or exposures are out of line with expectations or manager's stated style.
options.B: Style analysis is a one-time screening exercise performed only at initial hire.
options.C: Tracking style results over time serves mainly to recompute fees on style-adjusted benchmarks.

rationales.A:
Full statement: results of RBSA and HBSA should be tracked over time in order to ascertain if risk trends or exposures are out of line with expectations or manager's stated style; deviations may signal issues such as style drift are developing; style analysis is most useful with strategies that hold publicly traded securities where pricing is frequent; can be applied to other strategies (hedge funds and PE) but insights from style analysis of such strategies more likely to be used for designing additional lines of inquiry in due diligence rather than for confirmation of investment process.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The results of the returns-based style analysis and the holdings-based style analysis should be tracked over time in order to ascertain if the risk trends or exposures are out of line with expectations or the manager's stated style. Deviations may signal that issues, such as style drift, are developing.' And: 'Style analysis is most useful with strategies that hold publicly traded securities where pricing is frequent. It can be applied to other strategies (hedge funds and private equity, for example), but the insights drawn from a style analysis of such strategies are more likely to be used for designing additional lines of inquiry in the course of due diligence rather than for confirmation of the investment process.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. RBSA and HBSA are tracked THROUGH TIME to spot exposures drifting from expectations or mandate.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The purpose is monitoring risk trends and style drift, not fee arithmetic.

options.A: The positive asymmetry profile holds the S&P 500 at a constant 50% weight in all market environments.
options.B: positive asymmetry profile is allocated 75% to S&P 500 for months when S&P 500 return is positive and 25% when S&P 500 return is negative.
options.C: The profile allocates 25% to equities in up months and 75% in down months.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The profile is state-dependent — its weight varies with the sign of the index return.
rationales.B:
Full statement: positive asymmetry profile is allocated 75% to S&P 500 for months when S&P 500 return is positive and 25% when S&P 500 return is negative; remainder allocated to 90-day T-bills; results in UC 75%, DC 25%, capture ratio 3.0 (convex return profile).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The positive asymmetry profile is allocated 75% to the S&P 500 for months when the S&P 500 return is positive and 25% when the S&P 500 return is negative... The remainder for all profiles is allocated to 90-day T-bills.' Corresponds to Exhibit 4 UC 75%, DC 25%, Ratio 3.0.
rationales.C: Incorrect. That is the NEGATIVE asymmetry pattern; positive asymmetry holds 75% in up months and 25% in down months.

options.A: profiles chosen to illustrate potential effects of capture ratio and drawdown on return performance and highlight why understanding capture ratio and drawdown is important for manager selection.
options.B: Capture ratios and drawdown measure the same property, so using both is redundant.
options.C: Drawdown analysis replaces capture ratios entirely for asymmetric strategies.

rationales.A:
Full statement: profiles chosen to illustrate potential effects of capture ratio and drawdown on return performance and highlight why understanding capture ratio and drawdown is important for manager selection; asymmetrical return profile or avoiding large drawdowns particularly during periods when market is not trending strongly upward can result in higher risk-adjusted returns; time period 2000-2013 chosen specifically because it encompasses extreme drawdown of 2008-2009.

Correct. Per the reading: 'We designed these four trading strategies to illustrate the potential effects of the capture ratio and drawdown on return performance and to highlight why understanding the capture ratio and drawdown is important for manager selection.' And: 'An asymmetrical return profile or avoiding large drawdowns, particularly during periods when the market is not trending strongly upward, can result in higher risk-adjusted returns.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. They are complementary lenses — capture ratios describe up/down participation; drawdown describes loss depth and duration.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Neither replaces the other; together they reveal how return asymmetry and loss behavior interact.

options.A: Manager types cannot be organized by tracking risk because tracking risk is unobservable.
options.B: quadrants based on tracking risk (high vs low) and diversification (high vs low): sector rotation strategies (high tracking risk, low diversification).
options.C: Closet indexers occupy the high-tracking-risk, high-diversification quadrant.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Tracking risk is directly measurable and forms one axis of the quadrant framework.
rationales.B:
Full statement: quadrants based on tracking risk (high vs low) and diversification (high vs low): sector rotation strategies (high tracking risk, low diversification); concentrated stock pickers (high tracking risk, low diversification of stock picks); closet indexer (low tracking risk despite claiming to be active); diversified stock pickers (low tracking risk from many small active bets).

Correct. Per the reading Exhibit: 'Sector rotation... Concentrated stock pickers... Closet indexer... Diversified stock pickers.' The framework distinguishes managers based on tracking risk levels and portfolio diversification patterns.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Closet indexers sit in the LOW tracking risk quadrant; concentrated stock pickers occupy high tracking risk with low diversification.

options.A: behavioral inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by actions of other market participants, usually associated with biases such as trend following or loss aversion.
options.B: Behavioral and structural inefficiencies are a single category, since all mispricings trace to investor psychology.
options.C: Structural inefficiencies arise from investor biases such as trend following and loss aversion.

rationales.A:
Full statement: behavioral inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by actions of other market participants, usually associated with biases such as trend following or loss aversion; these inefficiencies are temporary, lasting long enough for manager to identify and exploit them before market price and perceived intrinsic value converge; structural inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by external or internal rules and regulations; these inefficiencies can be long lived and assume continuation of rules and regulations rather than convergence.

Correct. Per the reading: 'active strategies assume markets are sufficiently inefficient that security mispricings can be identified and exploited. These opportunities typically arise when market behavior deviates from the manager's fundamental assumptions. Generally speaking, inefficiencies can be categorized as behavioral or structural. Behavioral inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by the actions of other market participants, usually associated with biases, such as trend following or loss aversion. These inefficiencies are temporary, lasting long enough for the manager to identify and exploit them before the market price and perceived intrinsic value converge. Structural inefficiencies are perceived mispricings created by external or internal rules and regulations. These inefficiencies can be long lived and assume a continuation of the rules and regulations rather than a convergence.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The two are distinct — behavioral inefficiencies come from participant actions and biases; structural ones from rules, regulation, or market design.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Bias-driven mispricings are BEHAVIORAL; structural inefficiencies are created by external rules and market structure.

options.A: Capacity analysis considers only the manager's current assets under management.
options.B: An inefficiency's capacity is unlimited if the manager trades liquid large-cap securities.
options.C: level of assets strategy or opportunity can absorb without dilution of returns, number of opportunities or securities available, ability to transact in timely manner at or near market price (liquidity).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Capacity spans the assets the opportunity can absorb without return dilution, the number of opportunities, and transaction feasibility.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Even liquid strategies dilute as assets grow relative to the opportunity set.
rationales.C:
Full statement: level of assets strategy or opportunity can absorb without dilution of returns, number of opportunities or securities available, ability to transact in timely manner at or near market price (liquidity); key questions: does inefficiency provide sufficient frequency of opportunity and level of return to cover transaction costs and fees (if so, does this require leverage)?; does inefficiency provide repeatable source of return?; is inefficiency sustainable (function of market's depth and liquidity as well as how much capital allocated to inefficiency — more well-known inefficiency more likely to be arbitraged away).

Correct. Per the reading: 'If the source of return is linked to a credible inefficiency, there is the additional issue of capacity. Capacity has several related aspects, such as the level of assets the strategy or opportunity can absorb without a dilution of returns, the number of opportunities or securities available, and the ability to transact in a timely manner at or near the market price — that is, liquidity. Overall, capacity is the level, repeatability, and sustainability of returns that the inefficiency is expected to support in the future. Does the inefficiency provide a sufficient frequency of opportunity and level of return to cover transaction costs and fees? If so, does this require leverage? Does the inefficiency provide a repeatable source of return?... Is the inefficiency sustainable? That is, at what asset level would the realized return from the inefficiency be unacceptably low? Sustainability will be a function of the market's depth and liquidity, as well as how much capital is allocated, either by the manager or competitors, to the inefficiency. All else equal, the more well-known an inefficiency, the more likely it is to be arbitraged away.'

options.A: Portfolio construction evaluation is unnecessary when the manager's philosophy is well articulated.
options.B: The construction review is limited to verifying position counts against the prospectus.
options.C: how are portfolio allocations set and adjusted (allocation process should be consistent with investment philosophy and process.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Articulated philosophy must be TESTED against how allocations are actually set and adjusted — consistency between process and portfolio is the question.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The inquiry is substantive: how allocations are determined, adjusted, and kept consistent with the stated process.
rationales.C:
Full statement: how are portfolio allocations set and adjusted (allocation process should be consistent with investment philosophy and process; e.g., turnover should agree with frequency of signals, payoff horizon of signals, securities' liquidity)?; do portfolio allocations incorporate manager's conviction?; how have portfolio characteristics changed with asset growth?; does portfolio use stop-losses to manage risk (hard: positions automatically sold; soft: positions evaluated when threshold reached)?; what types of securities are used?

Correct. Per the reading: 'How are portfolio allocations set and adjusted? The allocation process should be consistent with investment philosophy and process. For example, if the portfolio is actively managed, its turnover should agree with the frequency of signals, the payoff horizon of the signals, and the securities' liquidity... Do portfolio allocations incorporate the manager's conviction (besides just the securities' risks and correlations with other securities in the portfolio)? How have the portfolio characteristics changed with asset growth? Has the number and/or characteristics of the positions held changed to accommodate a larger amount of AUM? Does the portfolio use stop-losses to manage risk? If so, are they hard (positions are automatically sold when the loss threshold is reached) or soft (positions are evaluated when the loss threshold is reached)?... What types of securities are used?'

options.A: does investment team have sufficient expertise and experience to effectively execute investment process (especially instructive to see how manager responded to stressed markets and poor performance)?.
options.B: Personnel evaluation is limited to confirming professional certifications and degrees.
options.C: Team assessment matters only for quantitative strategies where model expertise is essential.

rationales.A:
Full statement: does investment team have sufficient expertise and experience to effectively execute investment process (especially instructive to see how manager responded to stressed markets and poor performance)?; does team have sufficient depth?; what is level of key person risk (overreliance on individual whose departure would negatively affect strategy's performance)?; what agreements (non-compete) and incentives (ownership, bonus, pay) exist to retain and attract key employees?; what has been turnover of firm personnel (high turnover risks loss of institutional knowledge)?

Correct. Per the reading: 'Does the investment team have sufficient expertise and experience to effectively execute the investment process? The need for expertise is self-evident. The greater the experience, particularly managing the current strategy across market environments, the greater the confidence in the manager's ability to effectively execute the investment process. As noted with drawdowns, it is especially instructive to see how the manager responded to stressed markets and poor performance. Does the investment team have sufficient depth to effectively execute the investment process?... What is the level of key person risk? A strategy that is overly dependent on the judgment or particular skills of an individual or small team of people faces key person risk, an overreliance on an individual or individuals whose departure would negatively affect the strategy's performance. What kinds of agreements (e.g., non-compete) and incentives (ownership, bonus, pay) exist to retain and attract key employees to join and stay at the firm? What has been the turnover of firm personnel? High personnel turnover risks the loss of institutional knowledge and experience within the team.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Credentials are a fraction of it — the question is whether the team's expertise and experience can EXECUTE the process, including through personnel change.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Every strategy depends on its people; the evaluation applies universally.

options.A: Authority bias describes junior analysts overstating their own expertise to gain influence.
options.B: involves groups deferring to a group member that is a subject matter expert or in a position of authority (e.g., the senior-most group member).
options.C: Authority bias is the tendency of groups to reject expert opinion in favor of consensus.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The bias is the GROUP'S deference to the authority figure or subject-matter expert, not junior self-promotion.
rationales.B:
Full statement: involves groups deferring to a group member that is a subject matter expert or in a position of authority (e.g., the senior-most group member); example: research analyst recommends semiconductor equipment stock to team of three portfolio managers, one of whom is former technology research analyst and votes not to buy — the other two vote the same way, deferring to subject matter expertise of the other manager; while this may be right call, it undermines reason for team in the first place: synthesis of diverse perspectives and skills.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Authority bias involves groups deferring to a group member that is a subject matter expert or in a position of authority (e.g., the senior-most group member). For example, a research analyst may recommend a semiconductor equipment stock to a team of three portfolio managers. One of the portfolio managers is a former technology research analyst and votes to not buy the stock. The other two members vote the same way, deferring to the subject matter expertise of the other manager. While this may be the right call, it undermines the reason for a team in the first place: a synthesis of diverse perspectives and skills.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The failure runs toward, not against, the authority — the group defers rather than challenges.

options.A: Bike-shedding refers to committees spending excessive time on the most complex agenda items.
options.B: Parkinson's law of triviality describes work expanding to fill the time allotted.
options.C: well-known phenomenon in which disproportionate attention is given to trivial issues at expense of important but harder-to-grasp or contested topics.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The phenomenon is the reverse — disproportionate attention to TRIVIAL, easy-to-grasp issues at the expense of harder important ones.
rationales.B: Incorrect. That is Parkinson's LAW; the law of TRIVIALITY (bike-shedding) concerns attention flowing to trivial topics.
rationales.C:
Full statement: well-known phenomenon in which disproportionate attention is given to trivial issues at expense of important but harder-to-grasp or contested topics; also known as Parkinson's law of triviality or colloquially 'bike-shedding' — named after C. Northcote Parkinson's 1957 story of hypothetical committee organized to approve plans for nuclear power plant that spent more time discussing design of bicycle storage shed outside plant than design of nuclear reactor; survey of 120+ investment committees revealed committees spent great deal of time on issues they rated as unimportant at expense of important issues (asset allocation, capital market expectations, manager selection).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Aversion to complexity is a well-known phenomenon of groups in many professional contexts, in which disproportionate attention is given to trivial issues at the expense of important but harder-to-grasp or contested topics. This phenomenon is also known as Parkinson's law of triviality or, colloquially, "bike-shedding," named after C. Northcote Parkinson's 1957 memorable story of a hypothetical committee organized to approve plans for a nuclear power plant that spent more time discussing the design of a bicycle storage shed outside the plant than the design of the nuclear reactor.' And: 'A survey of more than 120 investment committees asked committee members to rank their tasks from most to least important and then asked the members to keep track of how they actually allocated their time. The survey revealed that the committees spent a great deal of time on issues they rated as unimportant, at the expense of important and more difficult issues, such as asset allocation, capital market expectations, and manager selection (Payne and Wood 2002).'

options.A: team size between three and five is optimal (larger teams introduce coordination issues and increase odds of groupthink per Patel and Sarkissian 2017).
options.B: Bias mitigation favors the largest possible teams, since more voices dilute any individual's bias.
options.C: The only effective mitigation is assigning all decisions to a single accountable individual.

rationales.A:
Full statement: team size between three and five is optimal (larger teams introduce coordination issues and increase odds of groupthink per Patel and Sarkissian 2017); using secret ballot may be preferable to open deliberation because reduces groupthink and authority bias; meeting agendas should be agreed on in advance with most important topics covered first and allotted longest time for discussion (to avoid complexity aversion and availability bias); diverse investment teams (in terms of social and cognitive categories) reduce groupthink and errors — CFA Institute long advocated for DEI in investment industry.

Correct. Per the reading: 'How large is the investment decision-making team? Research suggests that a team size between three and five is optimal, with larger teams introducing too many coordination issues (Patel and Sarkissian 2017) and increasing the odds of groupthink. How are decisions made? While it may be awkward, using a secret ballot may be preferable to open deliberation because it reduces groupthink and authority bias. How are meetings conducted? What makes it on the agenda? To avoid complexity aversion, as well as availability bias, meeting agendas should be agreed on in advance, with the most important topics covered first and allotted the longest time for discussion. Is the investment team diverse? Research suggests that team diversity — in terms of social and cognitive categories — can reduce groupthink and errors (analogous to how combinations of uncorrelated assets reduce portfolio volatility)... CFA Institute has long advocated for diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) in the investment industry.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Larger teams introduce coordination problems and RAISE groupthink odds; three to five members is the cited optimum.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Single-decider structures trade group biases for individual ones; structured small teams with diverse perspectives are the technique.

options.A: Team diversity increases groupthink because varied backgrounds impede shared understanding.
options.B: research suggests team diversity — in terms of social and cognitive categories — can reduce groupthink and errors analogous to how combinations of uncorrelated assets reduce portfolio volatility.
options.C: The diversity benefit has no analogy in portfolio theory and stands as an isolated empirical result.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Research suggests social and cognitive diversity REDUCES groupthink and error rates.
rationales.B:
Full statement: research suggests team diversity — in terms of social and cognitive categories — can reduce groupthink and errors analogous to how combinations of uncorrelated assets reduce portfolio volatility; members of diverse teams may devote more effort to tasks because less concerned with socially 'fitting in' and conflict avoidance which is common in homogeneous groups.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Is the investment team diverse? Research suggests that team diversity — in terms of social and cognitive categories — can reduce groupthink and errors (analogous to how combinations of uncorrelated assets reduce portfolio volatility) and that members of diverse teams may devote more effort to tasks because they are less concerned with socially "fitting in" and conflict avoidance, which is common in homogeneous groups. For these reasons and others, CFA Institute has long advocated for diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) in the investment industry.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The cited analogy is diversification — imperfectly correlated perspectives, like imperfectly correlated assets, reduce aggregate error.

options.A: Surveyed committees allocate their meeting time in close proportion to task importance.
options.B: No research exists on how investment committees actually allocate their time.
options.C: survey of more than 120 investment committees asked committee members to rank tasks from most to least important and then to keep track of how they actually allocated their time.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The survey found MISALIGNMENT — time flowed to less important tasks relative to members' own importance rankings.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The 120-committee survey is precisely such research, and it documented the misalignment.
rationales.C:
Full statement: survey of more than 120 investment committees asked committee members to rank tasks from most to least important and then to keep track of how they actually allocated their time; survey revealed committees spent great deal of time on issues they rated as unimportant at expense of important and more difficult issues such as asset allocation, capital market expectations, and manager selection (Payne and Wood 2002).

Correct. Per the reading: 'A survey of more than 120 investment committees asked committee members to rank their tasks from most to least important and then asked the members to keep track of how they actually allocated their time. The survey revealed that the committees spent a great deal of time on issues they rated as unimportant, at the expense of important and more difficult issues, such as asset allocation, capital market expectations, and manager selection (Payne and Wood 2002).'

options.A: Separately managed accounts offer no advantages over pooled vehicles beyond prestige.
options.B: ownership (investor owns individual securities directly, additional safety in liquidity event, decisions not influenced by redemption demand of other investors, clear legal ownership for recovery in bankruptcy).
options.C: The SMA's defining advantage is lower cost than every pooled alternative.

rationales.A: Incorrect. SMAs offer direct security ownership, insulation from other investors' redemptions, customization, and tax efficiency.
rationales.B:
Full statement: ownership (investor owns individual securities directly, additional safety in liquidity event, decisions not influenced by redemption demand of other investors, clear legal ownership for recovery in bankruptcy); customization (allows investor to potentially express individual constraints or preferences within portfolio); tax efficiency (potentially improved tax efficiency — in US investors pay taxes only on realized capital gains); transparency (real-time position-level detail to investor providing complete transparency and accurate attribution).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The advantages of SMA vehicles include the following: Ownership. In an SMA, the investor owns the individual securities directly. This approach provides additional safety should a liquidity event occur. Although the manager continues to make investment decisions, these decisions will not be influenced by the redemption or liquidity demand of other investors in the strategy. An SMA also provides clear legal ownership for the recovery of assets resulting from unforeseen events, such as bankruptcy or mismanagement. Customization. SMAs allow the investor to potentially express individual constraints or preferences within the portfolio... Tax efficiency. SMAs offer potentially improved tax efficiency — for example, in such jurisdictions as the United States, where investors pay taxes only on realized capital gains. Transparency. SMAs offer real-time, position-level detail to the investor, providing complete transparency and accurate attribution to the investor. Even if a pooled vehicle provides position-level detail, such information will likely be presented with a delay.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. SMAs are often COSTLIER to administer; their advantages are ownership, customization, and tax management, not cheapness.

options.A: Open-end mutual funds are the most liquid vehicles because they offer daily NAV redemption.
options.B: most liquid vehicles are closed-end funds and ETFs — as listed securities can be bought and sold intra-day and price received depends on trading volume and depth of fund.
options.C: Limited partnerships provide the greatest liquidity through quarterly redemption windows.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Daily NAV is less liquid than INTRA-DAY exchange trading — closed-end funds and ETFs trade continuously as listed securities.
rationales.B:
Full statement: most liquid vehicles are closed-end funds and ETFs — as listed securities can be bought and sold intra-day and price received depends on trading volume and depth of fund; obvious advantage is ease of trading although can be some price uncertainty for less liquid funds particularly when trying to buy or sell large number of shares; open-end funds are slightly less liquid providing daily liquidity but also price certainty (shares bought and sold at end-of-day NAV).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The most liquid vehicles are closed-end funds and ETFs. As listed securities, they can be bought and sold intra-day, and the price received will depend on the trading volume and depth of the fund. The obvious advantage of these funds is ease of trading, although there can be some price uncertainty for less liquid funds, particularly when trying to buy or sell a large number of shares. Open-end funds are slightly less liquid, providing daily liquidity but also price certainty; shares are bought and sold at the end-of-day NAV.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Partnerships sit at the ILLIQUID end — lockups and gates, not liquidity leadership.

options.A: The investment relationship rests on informal understandings rather than contract documents.
options.B: The IPS is the sole contractual document between investor and manager for all vehicle types.
options.C: prospectus, private placement memorandum, and/or limited partnership agreement are in essence contract between investor and manager, outlining each party's rights and responsibilities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The prospectus, private placement memorandum, and/or limited partnership agreement function as the contract defining rights and obligations.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The IPS governs advisory relationships; pooled and partnership vehicles are governed by their offering and partnership documents.
rationales.C:
Full statement: prospectus, private placement memorandum, and/or limited partnership agreement are in essence contract between investor and manager, outlining each party's rights and responsibilities; documents cover numerous topics but liquidity and fees are focus of evaluation; objective of decision maker is to determine whether liquidity and fee structure make manager suitable for investor's needs and 'best' manager for expressing particular portfolio need.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An additional and important aspect of manager selection is understanding the terms of the investment as presented in the prospectus, private placement memorandum, and/or limited partnership agreement. These documents are, in essence, the contract between the investor and the manager, outlining each party's rights and responsibilities. Although these documents cover numerous topics, this lesson focuses on liquidity and fees. The objective of the decision maker is to determine whether the liquidity and fee structure make the manager suitable for the investor's needs and the "best" manager for expressing a particular portfolio need.'

options.A: disadvantage of partnership liquidity terms is reduced flexibility to adjust portfolio allocations in light of changing market conditions or investor circumstances and reduced ability to meet unexpected liquidity needs.
options.B: Partnership liquidity terms carry no cost to investors because lockups are compensated by fee discounts.
options.C: Reduced liquidity in partnerships benefits investors by preventing them from panic selling.

rationales.A:
Full statement: disadvantage of partnership liquidity terms is reduced flexibility to adjust portfolio allocations in light of changing market conditions or investor circumstances and reduced ability to meet unexpected liquidity needs; advantage of such terms is they do lock up capital for longer horizons, allowing funds to take long-term views and hold less liquid securities (start-ups, buyouts, turnarounds, real estate, natural resources) with reduced risk of having to sell portfolio holdings at inopportune times; additional advantage apparent during 2008 financial crisis: limited liquidity imposes long horizon view on investors, reducing or removing their ability to overreact.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The obvious disadvantage of partnership liquidity terms is the reduced flexibility to adjust portfolio allocations in light of changing market conditions or investor circumstances, as well as the reduced ability to meet unexpected liquidity needs. The advantage of such terms is that they do lock up capital for longer horizons, allowing funds to take long-term views and hold less liquid securities — such as start-up companies, buyouts, turnarounds, real estate, or natural resources — with reduced risk of having to sell portfolio holdings at inopportune times in response to redemption requests. An additional advantage, which was apparent during the 2008 financial crisis, is that limited liquidity imposes this long horizon view on investors, reducing or removing their ability to overreact.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. No automatic fee compensation exists; the cost is real — reduced flexibility to adjust allocations as conditions or needs change.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Whatever behavioral silver lining exists, the reading identifies the flexibility loss as the disadvantage of the terms.

options.A: Fee structures are neutral plumbing with no influence on manager behavior or mandate acceptance.
options.B: fee structures can influence which managers will be willing to accept particular investment mandate and can strongly affect manager behavior.
options.C: The principal-agent problem in fees is fully solved by standard 2-and-20 structures.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Fee design shapes WHICH managers accept a mandate and HOW they behave once hired — the core principal-agent channel.
rationales.B:
Full statement: fee structures can influence which managers will be willing to accept particular investment mandate and can strongly affect manager behavior; economic theory suggests principal-agent problem is complicated by fact that agent's skills and actions are not fully visible to principal; although principals control asset availability, agents control both expenditure of effort and portfolio risk; agent and principal may have different preferences (different time horizons, agents might not view losses same way principals do); total performance to some extent beyond control of either party; managers motivated to work hard even without incentive fees to retain current clients and expand client base — incentives useful to help ensure managers routinely act in clients' best interest.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Fee structures can influence which managers will be willing to accept a particular investment mandate. They can also strongly affect manager behavior. Economic theory suggests that the principal–agent problem is complicated by the fact that an agent's skills and actions are not fully visible to the principal. Although principals control asset availability, agents control both their expenditure of effort and portfolio risk. Moreover, the agent and principal may have different preferences; each might care about different time horizons and agents might not view losses the same way that principals do. Finally, total performance is, to some extent, beyond the control of either party. As a result of these factors, the principal's and agent's interests may not be fully aligned. In reality, managers are motivated to work hard even without incentive fees because they want to retain current clients and expand their client base and pricing power. Incentives are useful, however, to help ensure that managers routinely act in their clients' best interest.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Performance fees create their own incentive distortions (risk-taking near thresholds); no standard structure eliminates the problem.

options.A: performance fees may include maximum and high-water mark (or clawback) features that protect investors from situations such as paying for current positive performance before negative effects of prior underperformance have been offset.
options.B: Maximum-fee and high-water-mark features exist to protect the manager's revenue stream in down years.
options.C: High-water marks require investors to pay twice for the same performance when returns recover.

rationales.A:
Full statement: performance fees may include maximum and high-water mark (or clawback) features that protect investors from situations such as paying for current positive performance before negative effects of prior underperformance have been offset; private equity, hedge fund, and real estate partnerships commonly earn performance fees on total returns and typically do not limit amount of performance fee; hedge funds commonly include high-water mark features.

Correct. Per the reading: 'These fees may include maximum and high-water mark (or clawback) features that protect investors from situations such as paying for current positive performance before the negative effects of prior underperformance have been offset. Private equity, hedge fund, and real estate partnerships commonly earn performance fees on total returns and typically do not limit the amount of the performance fee. Hedge funds commonly include high-water mark features.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. These features protect INVESTORS — capping fees and preventing payment for performance that merely recovers prior losses.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The high-water mark prevents exactly that double payment: no incentive fee until prior peaks are exceeded.

options.A: Bonus-style fees resemble a put option the investor holds against the manager's underperformance.
options.B: Fee structures cannot be characterized in option terms because fees are not tradable claims.
options.C: bonus-style fees are close equivalent of manager's call option on share of active return, for which base fee is strike price.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The manager holds the optionality — a CALL on a share of active return, not an investor put.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Tradability is irrelevant to the payoff analogy; the bonus fee's payoff profile is call-like with the base fee as strike.
rationales.C:
Full statement: bonus-style fees are close equivalent of manager's call option on share of active return, for which base fee is strike price; option payoff modified by maximum fee feature; graph illustrates three fee components: base fee, plus a long call option on active return with strike price equal to minimum (base) fee, minus another (less valuable) call option with strike price equal to maximum fee; based on option pricing theory, higher volatility leads to higher option value, which encourages managers to assume higher portfolio risk — this behavior has been observed in the marketplace.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Bonus-style fees are the close equivalent of a manager's call option on a share of active return, for which the base fee is the strike price. Consider Exhibit 9, which shows a familiar-looking option payoff pattern using the fee parameters defined in Exhibit 8. In this case, the option payoff is modified by a maximum fee feature. The graph illustrates three fee components: a 25 bps base fee, plus a long call option on active return with a strike price equal to the minimum (base) fee, minus another (less valuable) call option with a strike price equal to the maximum fee.' And: 'Based on option pricing theory, higher volatility leads to higher option value, which encourages managers to assume higher portfolio risk. This behavior has been observed in the marketplace.'

options.A: Performance-based fees serve no economic function beyond raising manager compensation.
options.B: if investment outcomes result from mix of skill and luck (probability distribution around positive mean alpha), then performance fees constitute risk sharing.
options.C: Risk sharing through performance fees eliminates the investor's exposure to manager luck.

rationales.A: Incorrect. With outcomes mixing skill and luck, performance fees SHARE outcome risk between investor and manager.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if investment outcomes result from mix of skill and luck (probability distribution around positive mean alpha), then performance fees constitute risk sharing; fee structures must be designed carefully to avoid favoring one party over other; performance-based fees work to align interests of managers and investors because both parties share in investment results; investors benefit by paying performance-based fees rather than standard fees when active returns are low; managers may work harder to earn performance-based fees inspiring term 'incentive based.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'If investment outcomes result from a mix of skill and luck (i.e., a probability distribution around a positive mean alpha), then performance fees constitute risk sharing. Fee structures must be designed carefully to avoid favoring one party over the other. Performance-based fees work to align the interests of managers and investors because both parties share in investment results. Investors benefit by paying performance-based fees, rather than standard fees, when active returns are low. Managers may work harder to earn performance-based fees, inspiring the term "incentive based."'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Sharing is partial — the investor still bears outcome variability; the fee reallocates part of it.

options.A: Empirical studies find no relationship between fee structure and realized performance.
options.B: Evidence shows performance-based fees correlate with lower alphas due to excessive risk taking.
options.C: empirical evidence suggests correlation between performance-based fees and higher alphas (also lower fees) for mutual funds and higher risk-adjusted returns for hedge funds (Elton, Gruber, and Blake 2003.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The evidence links performance-based fees with HIGHER alphas (and lower total fees) in mutual funds and better risk-adjusted hedge fund results.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The documented correlation runs positive for alpha, not negative.
rationales.C:
Full statement: empirical evidence suggests correlation between performance-based fees and higher alphas (also lower fees) for mutual funds and higher risk-adjusted returns for hedge funds (Elton, Gruber, and Blake 2003; Ackermann, McEnally, and Ravenscraft 1999); asset managers may consider performance-based fees attractive because provide opportunity to enhance profits on upside and ensure guaranteed although perhaps minimal streams of revenue from base fees when performance is poor.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Empirical evidence suggests a correlation between performance-based fees and higher alphas (also, lower fees) for mutual funds and higher risk-adjusted returns for hedge funds. Asset managers may consider performance-based fees attractive because such fees provide an opportunity to enhance profits on the upside and ensure guaranteed, although perhaps minimal, streams of revenue from base fees when performance is poor.'

options.A: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — Adebayo's decision to invest 'quickly' without apparent analysis is made based on POLITICAL, not investment or financial, considerations.
options.B: There is no violation because sovereign investment decisions are government policy choices outside the Code.
options.C: Adebayo violated only Standard III(C) Suitability, since the urban project fits NI's socioeconomic mandate.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — Adebayo's decision to invest 'quickly' without apparent analysis is made based on POLITICAL, not investment or financial, considerations; Adebayo is facing political pressures from senior governmental supporters of each project; she must thoroughly and independently investigate each potential investment and be resolute in maintaining her independence and objectivity to act in the best interests of NI and its investors.

Correct. Per SIC Case Solution: 'Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis requires members to exercise diligence, independence, and thoroughness, as well as to have a reasonable and adequate basis to support investment action. Adebayo's decision to invest "quickly" without apparent analysis is made based on political, not investment or financial, considerations. Adebayo is facing political pressures from senior governmental supporters of each project. She must thoroughly and independently investigate each potential investment and be resolute in maintaining her independence and objectivity to act in the best interests of NI and its investors.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. NI's investment staff remain bound by the Standards; a quick, politically driven decision without analysis fails V(A).
rationales.C: Incorrect. The failure is DILIGENCE — deciding without investment analysis — not mandate fit.

options.A: Standard III(C) Suitability — by developing and recommending a balanced portfolio for the Kochanskis WITHOUT KNOWING their clients' investment objectives, risk tolerance, horizon, and so on.
options.B: There is no violation because a balanced portfolio is inherently reasonable for a retired couple.
options.C: The failure is one of performance presentation under Standard III(D), since the meeting centered on fund returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — by developing and recommending a balanced portfolio for the Kochanskis WITHOUT KNOWING their clients' investment objectives, risk tolerance, horizon, and so on; they must make a reasonable inquiry into a client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints PRIOR TO making any investment recommendation; they should have developed an INVESTMENT POLICY STATEMENT for the Kochanskis before they recommended any investments.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'By developing and recommending a balanced portfolio for the Kochanskis without knowing their clients' investment objectives, risk tolerance, horizon, and so on, Hockett and Lopez violated Standard III(C) Suitability... Hockett and Lopez should have developed an investment policy statement for the Kochanskis before they recommended any investments.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reasonableness in the abstract cannot substitute for KNOWING the clients' objectives, constraints, and experience before recommending.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The recommendation-before-inquiry sequence is the III(C) violation tested here.

options.A: Using an average of the four funds' returns to represent the model portfolio's performance was the violation.
options.B: Excluding terminated accounts — Lopez violated Standard III(D) Performance Presentation in her use and presentation of the composite portfolio's performance because she did NOT include the performance of TERMINATED ACCOUNTS.
options.C: Selecting discretionary accounts of similar size to build the composite was the violation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. For this novice audience, an average of the four funds is an acceptable simplification — the audience-tailoring principle.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Excluding terminated accounts — Lopez violated Standard III(D) Performance Presentation in her use and presentation of the composite portfolio's performance because she did NOT include the performance of TERMINATED ACCOUNTS; Lopez should have included terminated AND current discretionary accounts that were similar in her composite.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'Lopez violated Standard III(D) Performance Presentation in her use and presentation of the composite portfolio's performance because she did not include the performance of terminated accounts. In addition, she included only the team's discretionary accounts of similar size to the Kochanskis'. Lopez should have included terminated and current discretionary accounts that were similar in her composite portfolio's performance. There is no need to include nondiscretionary accounts because clients make their own investment decisions for such accounts.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Similar-size discretionary accounts are an appropriate composite population; the defect is EXCLUDING TERMINATED accounts, inflating the record.

options.A: There is no violation because high-beta stocks in modest proportion reduce overall portfolio risk through diversification.
options.B: Hockett's conduct violates only Standard III(D) if the alpha is presented to prospects.
options.C: Standard III(C) Suitability — Hockett is investing in high-beta stocks for client accounts with LONG-TERM OBJECTIVES in order to achieve his short-term quarterly performance bonus.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The 15% high-beta sleeve serves HIS short-term alpha objective, not the accounts' long-term conservative objectives — a suitability breach.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Presentation aside, investing inconsistently with client IPSs violates III(C) directly.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard III(C) Suitability — Hockett is investing in high-beta stocks for client accounts with LONG-TERM OBJECTIVES in order to achieve his short-term quarterly performance bonus; per Standard III(C), when responsible for managing a portfolio to a specific mandate, strategy, or style, must make only investment recommendations or take only investment actions that are CONSISTENT with the stated objectives and constraints; high-beta stocks by their very nature HAVE RISK; the high-beta stocks are contrary to the long-term risk profile established in the clients' IPSs; Hockett changed the investment strategy in an attempt to gain short-term rewards from firm's new compensation arrangement and not in response to changes in investment needs.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'Hockett is violating Standard III(C) Suitability by investing in high-beta stocks for client accounts with long-term objectives in order to achieve his short-term quarterly performance bonus... High-beta stocks by their very nature have risk. Hockett is also violating Standard III(C) by investing 15%-20% of his clients' portfolios in high-beta stocks that "appear low risk." These high-beta stocks are contrary to the long-term risk profile established in the clients' investment policy statements (IPSs). Hockett changed the investment strategy for his clients in an attempt to reap short-term rewards offered by his firm's new compensation arrangement and not in response to changes in their investment needs.'

options.A: Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality — members must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential unless: (1) info concerns illegal activities.
options.B: There is no violation because the post revealed no investment details or account balances.
options.C: There is no violation because the audience was limited to old high school friends rather than the public.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality — members must keep information about current, former, and prospective clients confidential unless: (1) info concerns illegal activities; (2) disclosure is required by law; or (3) the client permits disclosure; Standard III(E) requires that EVEN THE PRESENCE OF A CLIENT ACCOUNT be kept confidentially within the firm unless the client has been informed and has consented to the disclosure of the information; confidentiality extends BEYOND the investment industry to the population as a whole.

Correct. Per BankGlobal Case Solution: 'Lopez violated Standard III(E) Preservation of Confidentiality by posting about the Kochanskis' opening of an account... Standard III(E) requires that even the presence of a client account be kept confidentially within the firm unless the client has been informed and has consented to the disclosure of the information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The EXISTENCE of the client relationship is itself confidential information under III(E).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Audience size does not matter; disclosure to anyone outside authorized colleagues breaches confidentiality.

options.A: There is no violation because the ownership relationship is publicly disclosed in the subsidiaries' prospectuses.
options.B: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — by not disclosing that Global is owned by Castle in his research report, Plume violates VI(A).
options.C: The failure is one of independence under Standard I(B), since Castle's ownership pressures Plume's conclusions.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Public availability elsewhere does not satisfy VI(A) — the conflict must be disclosed IN THE RESEARCH REPORT itself.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — by not disclosing that Global is owned by Castle in his research report, Plume violates VI(A); CB's ownership of Global and Plume's close relationships with CB's management raise questions about Plume's ability to remain independent and objective; the fact that relationships are disclosed in offering prospectuses is NECESSARY BUT NOT SUFFICIENT to meet Standard VI(A) because they may not communicate the information effectively or in a prominent manner.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By not disclosing that Global is owned by Castle in his research report, Plume is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts... CB's ownership of Global and Plume's close relationships with CB's management raise questions about Plume's ability to remain independent and objective... The fact that the relationships between Plume, CB, its subsidiaries, and Global are disclosed in the offering prospectus is necessary but not sufficient to meet Standard VI(A) because they may not communicate the information effectively or in a prominent manner.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Pressure may exist, but the tested defect is the missing conflict disclosure in his reports.

options.A: There is no violation because Plume owns no shares of STRX or APBX, the companies he covers.
options.B: There is no violation because preferred shares and options confer no beneficial interest in the subsidiaries.
options.C: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Plume's ownership of CB's preferred shares and stock options, along with CB's voting control of each company, gives him a BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP INTEREST in STRX and APBX.

rationales.A: Incorrect. His CB securities plus CB's voting control of the subsidiaries create an INDIRECT beneficial interest in the covered companies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The indirect pecuniary link through the parent is precisely what VI(A) requires him to disclose.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts — Plume's ownership of CB's preferred shares and stock options, along with CB's voting control of each company, gives him a BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP INTEREST in STRX and APBX; for the purposes of Standard VI(A), members and candidates BENEFICIALLY OWN securities if they have a DIRECT OR INDIRECT PECUNIARY INTEREST, have the POWER TO DIRECT VOTING, or have the POWER TO DISPOSE.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By not disclosing his ownership of CB's preferred shares and stock options in his research report, Plume is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts. Because of this ownership in CB, Plume has a beneficial ownership interest in the securities of STRX and APBX, both of which he recommended as "buys." The most obvious conflict that must always be disclosed involves relationships between an issuer and the member, the candidate, or his or her firm. For the purposes of Standard VI(A), members and candidates beneficially own securities or other investments if they have a direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the securities, have the power to direct the voting of the shares of the securities or investments, or have the power to dispose of or direct the disposition of the security or investment.'

options.A: Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity — members must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity.
options.B: There is no violation because bonus arrangements tied to firm revenues are standard sell-side compensation.
options.C: The arrangement implicates only Standard VI(A) and is cured by disclosure in the research reports.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity — members must use reasonable care and judgment to achieve and maintain independence and objectivity; must NOT accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that reasonably could be expected to COMPROMISE independence and objectivity; compensation arrangements should NOT LINK analyst remuneration DIRECTLY to investment banking assignments in which the analysts may participate as a team member; Global can avoid the conflict by REMOVING this incentive from Plume's compensation package.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By not disclosing his annual bonus in his research report, Plume is in violation of Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity... The guidance for Standard I(B) states, "Compensation arrangements should not link analyst remuneration directly to investment banking assignments in which the analysts may participate as a team member." The guidance also states, "Having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate only when the conflicts are adequately and effectively managed and disclosed... Firms should require public disclosure of actual conflicts of interest to investors." Global can avoid the conflict by removing this incentive from Plume's compensation package.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. A bonus tied to IPO proceeds he works on gives Plume a direct stake in banking outcomes — compromising research independence under I(B).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure helps, but compensation structurally linked to banking success attacks independence itself — I(B) is the operative standard.

options.A: There is no violation because analysts may manage personal portfolios according to their own risk views.
options.B: Standard VI(A) — selling APBX short in his personal portfolio while having a 'buy' recommendation on the stock is a conflict of interest that Plume needs to disclose.
options.C: There is no violation because a short position is not beneficial ownership of the security.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Holding a personal SHORT against his own outstanding BUY recommendation is a conflict that must be disclosed — personal risk views do not erase it.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) — selling APBX short in his personal portfolio while having a 'buy' recommendation on the stock is a conflict of interest that Plume needs to disclose; Global could avoid this conflict by PROHIBITING such transactions by its employees; Plume's ownership of CB's preferred shares and common stock options, along with CB's voting control, gives him a beneficial ownership interest in APBX.

Correct. Per Castle Bio 1 Solution: 'By selling APBX short in his personal portfolio, Plume is in violation of Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts... Selling APBX short in his personal portfolio while having a "buy" recommendation on the stock is a conflict of interest that Plume needs to disclose.
